summary:
Patient Movement & Lifting

Body Mechanics for EMS Providers — Protecting Your Most Important Tool

Musculoskeletal injuries are the most common occupational injury among EMS providers. Lifting, moving, and carrying patients — often in awkward positions, confined spaces, or with heavy patients — puts immense strain on the spine. Proper body mechanics are not optional; they are essential for a long, injury-free career. The principles are simple but must be practiced until automatic:

  • Lift with your legs, not your back: The powerful quadriceps and gluteal muscles are designed for lifting. The lower back muscles are not. Let your legs do the work.
  • Keep your back straight (neutral spine): Maintain the natural S-curve of your spine. Do not hunch, arch, or twist. A straight back distributes the load evenly.
  • Feet shoulder-width apart: A wide base of support provides stability. Being too narrow can cause loss of balance.
  • Keep the load close to your body: The farther the load extends from your center of gravity, the greater the torque on your spine. Holding 75 kg at arms length creates 10x the spinal load of holding it close.
  • Pivot, do not twist: When turning with a load, move your feet — do not twist your torso. Twisting while lifting is one of the fastest ways to herniate a disc.
Critical Warning: Over 50% of EMS providers will experience a career-ending back injury if they do not practice proper lifting mechanics. If a patient is too heavy to lift safely with available resources, wait for additional help — do not attempt to lift beyond your physical capacity. No call is worth a career-ending injury.

Power Lift (Squat Lift)

The power lift is the standard lifting technique for stretchers, backboards, and equipment. It maximizes use of leg muscles and minimizes spinal stress: stand feet shoulder-width apart, one foot slightly ahead; squat by bending at hips and knees (NOT at waist); keep back straight; grip with palms facing up (power grip); bring load close to body; lift by extending hips and legs; breathe out during the lift (exhale on exertion to prevent Valsalva).

Power Grip

The power grip uses the entire hand rather than just fingers. Palms face up (supinated), fingers and thumb wrap around the handle or frame. Wrists stay straight (neutral alignment). This provides significantly more grip strength than a pinch grip and ensures better control of the load.

Team Lifting Communication

  • Designate a leader — typically the EMS provider at the head of the stretcher.
  • Use a countdown: On three — one, two, three — lift! Everyone lifts on lift, not three.
  • Confirm readiness before the count — each rescuer responds ready.
  • Lower together using the same countdown pattern.
  • Verbalize the plan — destination, obstacles, who calls commands.

Equipment for Lifting and Moving

DeviceBest UseSpecial Considerations
Wheeled StretcherPrimary transport deviceLoad capacity 350-650 lb. Lock wheels before transfer. Secure with straps.
Scoop StretcherPatients on ground, splits to slide underGood for suspected spinal injury. Lacks lateral stability — secure to backboard.
Basket Stretcher (Stokes)Technical rescue, rough terrainUse with patient harness for vertical lifts. Foot end first in steep terrain.
Reeves SleeveConfined spaces, narrow hallwaysFull-body wrap. Multiple handles. Can be dragged in emergency.
Stair ChairMoving up/down stairsPatient must sit upright. Secure all straps. Never bariatric with only 2 rescuers.
Long BackboardSpinal immobilization, extricationNow used less for transport (pressure points, respiratory restriction). Pad generously.

Stair Chair — Proper Technique

Moving patients up or down stairs is one of the most physically demanding and hazardous EMS operations. Going DOWN stairs: patient goes feet first, the rescuer at the foot bears the majority of the weight, the rescuer at the head controls the descent, both keep backs straight and use legs. Going UP stairs: patient goes head first, the head rescuer bears more weight. Coordinate every step: Next step — ready, step. Never attempt bariatric stair transport with only two rescuers.

Helicopter / Air Medical Landing Zone

RequirementSpecification
Minimum LZ size100 ft x 100 ft (30 m x 30 m), clear of trees, wires, poles, debris
SurfaceFirm, level, dry. Avoid loose gravel (rotor wash creates blinding dust). Slopes < 5-10°
MarkingWeighted cones with lights at all 4 corners. Weights prevent rotor wash from blowing them.
Approach directionApproaches and departs INTO the wind. Communicate wind direction to pilot.
Night operationsMark LZ with lights. Do NOT point flashlights at helicopter. Turn off vehicle headlights if directed.
Ground personnelStay clear during landing/takeoff. Approach only when signaled. Approach from FRONT at 45° toward pilot.
Sloped terrainApproach from downhill side. Pilot can see you better from uphill side.
Patient handoffBring patient only when signaled. Never walk under tail boom. Follow pilot directions.
Helicopter Safety: NEVER approach a helicopter from behind. The tail rotor is nearly invisible when spinning, especially at night. It has no guard. It will kill you. Always approach from the front at a 45° angle toward the pilot, making eye contact. Crouch low when approaching. Do not carry anything above your head. Secure all loose equipment.

Bariatric Patient Movement

Additional rescuers (4+), bariatric-rated equipment (wider stretchers, higher capacity cots up to 800+ lb, reinforced backboards), mechanical lift equipment when available, careful assessment of environment, and recognition of personal lifting limits. If the patient cannot be safely moved, call the fire department for additional personnel. Never attempt alone.

Ongoing Lift Assessment

Before any lift, ask yourself: Can I safely lift this patient with available help? Am I fatigued? Is the environment safe (floor condition, space, terrain)? If the answer to any is no — call for additional resources. It is better to wait 5 minutes for help than to spend 6 months recovering from a herniated disc.

quiz.q: An EMS provider lifts a backboard bending at the waist, legs straight, palm-down finger grip. Which injury prevention principles is he violating?
options: All three: not using legs, not straight back, not power grip
Bending at waist is acceptable if back is straight

quiz.q: Transporting 90 kg patient down three flights of narrow stairs in stair chair. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: Going down, patient head first so head rescuer can see
Going down, patient feet first. Foot rescuer bears most weight. Both use leg-driven lifting
Both rescuers face same direction toward patient
Only two rescuers needed regardless of patient weight

quiz.q: [HARD] Night helicopter LZ. Wind shifts 180 degrees from original direction. Pilot needs to approach into wind. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Leave cones, pilot can see LZ regardless of wind
Relocate cones if needed, radio current wind direction. Step clear of LZ during landing
Run to center of LZ with flashlight to guide pilot
Turn on all vehicle headlights pointed at LZ

quiz.q: A bariatric patient weighing 180 kg needs supine transport down narrow stairs. Patient cannot sit upright. What is the BEST approach?
options: Reeves sleeve or bariatric dragging device with 4-6 rescuers. Stair chair not appropriate for supine patient. Call fire department
Patient can walk downstairs with assistance
Two rescuers can manage with reinforced backboard

flashcards.question: What are the key principles of proper body mechanics?
flashcards.answer: 1) Lift with legs, not back. 2) Back straight (neutral spine). 3) Feet shoulder-width apart. 4) Load close to body. 5) Pivot, don't twist.

flashcards.question: What is the power lift (squat lift) technique?
flashcards.answer: Squat at hips/knees (not waist), back straight, palms-up power grip, load close, extend legs to lift, exhale on exertion.

flashcards.question: What is the power grip and why use it?
flashcards.answer: Palms up, entire hand around object. Provides maximum grip strength and control vs finger-only grip.

flashcards.question: How should a stair chair carry be performed going down stairs?
flashcards.answer: Patient feet first. Foot rescuer bears most weight. Head rescuer controls descent. Both keep backs straight, use legs. Coordinate each step.

flashcards.question: What are the minimum landing zone requirements for a helicopter?
flashcards.answer: 100x100 ft minimum, firm, level, clear of debris. Weighted lighted cones at corners. Approach from front at 45°, NEVER from behind.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between predetermined and direct carry?
flashcards.answer: Predetermined: stretcher pre-raised, less bending. Direct: stretcher at ground level, full squat lift. Predetermined preferred when possible.

flashcards.question: What special considerations for bariatric patient movement?
flashcards.answer: 4+ rescuers, bariatric-rated equipment, mechanical lifts, careful environment assessment, recognize personal limits. Call fire department early.

flashcards.question: What is correct team lifting communication?
flashcards.answer: Designate leader. Confirm readiness. Countdown: On three — one, two, three — lift! Lift on lift. Same pattern for lowering.

critical.scenario: Third-floor walk-up. 68-year-old 100 kg female with CHF exacerbation. SpO2 89% on room air. Cannot walk due to dyspnea. Narrow stairs with turn. You and your smaller partner are both fatigued from carrying equipment up. Partner says just us in the stair chair, it is only three floors.
critical.question: What is the MOST appropriate plan?

options.t: Proceed with stair chair. Patient needs rapid transport for CHF. Two rescuers can handle 100 kg with proper body mechanics
options.f: Incorrect Two fatigued rescuers with 100 kg patient on narrow stairs with turn is high-risk. Fatigue significantly increases injury risk.

options.t: Call for additional manpower (fire department). While waiting, provide O2, position of comfort, monitor. If patient deteriorates to life-threatening, use dragging device for emergency evacuation
options.f: Correct Provider fatigue, patient weight (100 kg), narrow stairs with turn, smaller partner. Factors arguing for extra help. Patient condition serious but not immediately life-threatening. Call fire department for lift assistance.

critical.explanation: Factors for extra help: provider fatigue from stair climb, patient weight (100 kg), narrow stairs with turn, smaller partner. CHF with hypoxia is time-sensitive but not immediately life-threatening (alert, O2 responsive). Calling fire department for lift assistance is standard practice. Always err on side of calling for help when unsure about lift safety.
critical.kpi: Recognizes when additional lifting resources are needed based on patient weight, environmental constraints, and provider fatigue

critical.scenario: Night LZ established for helicopter trauma transport. 120x120 ft field with gentle 8-10° slope downward to south. Wind from north at 10 mph. Power line runs along one fence line.
critical.question: How should the LZ be set up and approach made?

options.t: Mark all four corners with cones. Helicopter approaches from north INTO wind. Note power line hazard. After landing, approach from front at 45°, NEVER from behind
options.f: Correct Approach into wind (north). LZ adequate size. Power line is significant hazard. After landing: approach from front 45°, never behind tail rotor.

options.t: Mark only two corners facing wind. Helicopters can land any direction. Power line at edge is not a concern. Approach from whichever side is closest
options.f: Incorrect Must land into wind. Mark all four corners. Power lines significant hazard especially at night. Never approach from behind.

critical.explanation: LZ safety rules: minimum 100x100 ft, approach INTO wind, power lines major hazard mark or reposition, mark all four corners with weighted lighted cones, after landing wait for pilot signal and approach from front at 45° making eye contact, never walk behind tail boom, secure all loose equipment.
critical.kpi: Correctly establishes night helicopter LZ with wind direction, marking, hazard awareness, and approach safety

critical.scenario: Third floor. 72-year-old male fell, no trauma, stable vitals, ~75 kg. Building has elevator. Halfway to elevator with stair chair, your partner feels sharp back pain and cannot continue lifting.
critical.question: What should the EMS provider do?

options.t: Continue alone carefully lower stair chair and drag patient to elevator. Partner can rest and recover
options.f: Incorrect Continuing alone puts both you and patient at risk. Cannot safely manage 100+ kg patient+chair alone in narrow hallway.

options.t: Stop immediately. Lower stair chair to stabilize it. Assess partner injury. Call dispatch for additional resources. Reassure patient help is coming. Wait for additional personnel
options.f: Correct When lifting partner becomes injured mid-transport: stop unsafe lift, lower and stabilize chair, assess partner, call for resources, reassure patient, wait for additional crew.

critical.explanation: Partner injuries during lifts are a known risk in EMS. Correct response: 1) Stop the unsafe lift immediately lower and stabilize stair chair. 2) Assess partner back injury. 3) Call dispatch for additional resources. 4) Reassure patient. 5) Wait for additional crew. Do NOT attempt to complete lift alone. Document the partner injury per service protocol.
critical.kpi: Responds appropriately to partner lifting injury by stabilizing patient, calling for resources, and avoiding further injury
